The State of Urban Cycling Infrastructure

By Maria Ostrowski · July 12, 2026 · 9 min read

Over the past decade, dozens of cities have rewired their street networks to move people on bicycles instead of in cars. The results are uneven: some networks are contiguous and useful, others are aspirational paint on dangerous roads. The difference between a network that transforms a city and one that merely decorates it comes down to a small number of design choices.

This feature walks through four of them — protected lanes, intersection treatment, network continuity, and winter maintenance — and shows why each one alone is insufficient. A city that builds any one of these without the others tends to end up with infrastructure that looks impressive in renderings but does not move the modal share.

Protected lanes, not painted lanes

A painted lane between moving traffic and the curb is not infrastructure; it is a suggestion. The decisive variable is vertical separation: a kerb, a row of planters, or parked cars between the cyclist and moving traffic. Cities that built protected networks saw cycling rates double or triple within a decade; cities that merely painted lanes saw no measurable change.

The table below summarizes the outcomes in three comparable cities over a ten-year window:

CityLane typeCycling mode-share change (10y)
HelsinkiProtected+12 points
LyonProtected+9 points
AustinPainted+1 point

Intersection treatment

Most car-cyclist collisions happen at intersections, not mid-block. Protected lanes that vanish at the intersection are worse than useless: they route cyclists into the most dangerous part of the road without protection. The treatments that work — raised corner islands, advance stop lines, separate signal phases — are not exotic. They are just rare.

Network continuity

A network is only as useful as its weakest link. A cyclist who feels safe for ninety percent of a trip and terrified for the remaining ten will drive instead. Continuity is the hardest problem in cycling infrastructure because it requires taking space from cars or parking on every block, not just the easy ones. Cities that solved continuity saw mode share move; cities that did not, did not.

Winter maintenance

A network that is unusable for four months of the year is half a network. Cold-climate cities that prioritized winter clearing — dedicated ploughs for bike lanes, brine pre-treatment, and the same service-level standard as arterial roads — saw winter cycling rates that matched summer rates in cities without it. Winter cycling is not a behaviour problem; it is a maintenance problem.
